quality.rationale: 内外部度规参数化匹配+多步外曲率张量计算+理想恒星坍缩物理模型

origin.question: [文件 1: 1_s16_16.30_iii.md]Problem 16.30. Consider the gravitational collapse of a spherically symmetric, perfect fluid star of zero pressure and uniform density (i.e. uniform throughout the star as seen by observers comoving with the fluid).(iii) Show that the Friedmann and Schwarzschild metrics match together smoothly at the surface of the star. (It is necessary and sufficient to show that the intrinsic 3-geometry of the surface and the extrinsic curvature of the surface are the same whether measured in the exterior or the interior.)## Solution(iii) We shall do the matching for $k = +1$ ; the cases $k = 0$ or $k = -1$ are similar. For $k = +1$ , Equations (2) and (3) give$$\left(a, r\right)^{2} = a_{\mathrm {m}} / a - 1 \tag {5}$$where the constant in Equation (3) has been fixed by setting $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{a}_{\mathfrak{m}} =$ maximum of a, when $\mathbf{a}_{,r} = 0$ . Equation (5) can be integrated as it stands; it is convenient, however, to introduce a new time parameter by$$\mathrm {d} r = \operatorname {a d} \eta . \tag {6}$$Equations (5) and (6) then give$$\begin{array}{l}a = \frac {1}{2} a_{m} (1 + \cos \eta) (7) \\r = \frac {1}{2} a_{\mathrm {m}} (\eta + \sin \eta). (8) \\ \end{array}$$The constants of integration have been chosen to make $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{m}}$ and $r = 0$ at $\eta = 0$ , and to make $\mathbf{a} = 0$ and $r = \pi \mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{m}} / 2$ at $\eta = \pi$ .The intrinsic 3-geometry of the surface of the star, as measured from the interior, is found by putting $\chi = \chi_0$ in Equation (1):$$\begin{array}{l}\left.^{(3)} d s^{2} = - d \tau^{2} + a^{2} (\tau) \sin^{2} \chi_{0} d \Omega^{2}\right. \\= a^{2} (\eta) \left(- d \eta^{2} + \sin^{2} \chi_{0} d \Omega^{2}\right). \tag {9} \\ \end{array}$$The exterior metric is$$\mathrm {d s}^{2} = - (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm{r}) \mathrm{d t}^{2} + (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm{r})^{- 1} \mathrm{d r}^{2} + \mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{d} \Omega^{2}. \tag {10}$$The surface of the star is at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{R}(r)$ , and from the equations for radial geodesics (see Problem 15.4), $\mathbf{R}(r)$ is given by$$R = \frac {1}{2} R_{i} (1 + \cos \eta) \tag {11}$$$$\tau = \left(\frac {\mathrm {R}_{\mathrm {i}}^{3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}} (\eta + \sin \eta) \tag {12}$$$$u^{t} = \frac {d t}{d r} = \frac {\left(1 - 2 M / R_{i}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}}{1 - 2 M / R}. \tag {13}$$Thus the geometry of the surface is$$\begin{array}{l}\left(^{(3)} \mathrm {d} s_{+}^{2} = - \mathrm {d} \tau^{2} + R^{2} (r) \mathrm{d} \Omega^{2}\right. \\= - \left(\frac {\mathrm {R}_{\mathrm {i}}^{3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) (1 + \cos \eta)^{2} \mathrm {d} \eta^{2} + \frac {\mathrm {R}_{\mathrm {i}}^{2}}{4} (1 + \cos \eta)^{2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^{2}. \tag {14} \\ \end{array}$$Comparison with Equation (9) shows that the 3-geometries match provided we identify$$\mathrm {R}_{\mathrm {i}} = \mathrm {a}_{\mathrm {m}} \sin \chi_{0} , \quad 2 \mathrm {M} = \mathrm {a}_{\mathrm {m}} \sin^{3} \chi_{0} . \tag {1 5}$$Now compute the extrinsic curvature $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{ij}}^{(-)}$ in the interior. The normal to the surface is$$\mathbf {n} = \mathbf {a}^{- 1} \partial / \partial \chi \tag {1 6}$$(recall that $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{n} = 1$ ), while the vectors $\mathbf{u} = \partial/\partial \tau$ , $\partial/\partial \theta$ and $\partial/\partial \phi$ lie in the surface. Let the indices $i, j$ range over $\tau, \theta, \phi$ . Then$$\begin{array}{l}\mathrm {K}_{\mathrm {i j}} \equiv - \mathrm {e}_{\mathrm {i}} \cdot \nabla_{\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {n} = - \mathrm {e}_{\mathrm {i}} \cdot \Gamma_{\mathrm {n j}}^{\alpha} \mathrm {e}_{\alpha} = - \mathrm {g}_{\mathrm {i \alpha}} \Gamma_{\mathrm {n j}}^{\alpha} \\= - \Gamma_{\text {i n j}} = - \frac {1} {2} \left( \mathrm {g}_{\text {i n} , \mathrm {j}} + \mathrm {g}_{\mathrm {ij} , \mathrm {n}} - \mathrm {g}_{\mathrm {nj} , \mathrm {i}}\right) = - \frac {1} {2} \mathrm {g}_{\mathrm {ij} , \mathrm {n}} \tag {1 7} \\ \end{array}$$since $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{in}} = \mathbf{a}^{-1}\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{i}\chi} = 0$ . From the metric we see that$$\mathrm {K}_{\tau \tau}^{( - )} = \mathrm {K}_{\tau \theta}^{( - )} \dot eq \mathrm {K}_{\tau \phi}^{( - )} = \mathrm {K}_{\theta \phi}^{( - )} = 0 \tag {1 8}$$$$\mathrm {K}_{\theta \theta}^{( - )} = \mathrm {K}_{\phi \phi}^{( - )} \sin^{- 2} \theta = - \frac {1} {2} \mathrm {a}_{\mathrm {m}} ( 1 + \cos \eta ) \sin \chi_{0} \cos \chi_{0} . \tag {1 9}$$In the exterior metric, the 4-velocity is$$\mathbf {u} = \mathbf {u}^{\mathbf {t}} \mathbf {e}_{\mathbf {t}} + \mathbf {u}^{\mathbf {r}} \mathbf {e}_{\mathbf {r}} .$$The normal vector$$\mathbf {n} = \mathbf {n}^{\mathrm {t}} \mathbf {e}_{\mathrm {t}} + \mathbf {n}^{\mathrm {r}} \mathbf {e}_{\mathrm {r}}$$satisfies$$\mathbf {n} \cdot \mathbf {n} = 1 = \mathrm {g}_{\mathrm {tt}} \left( \mathrm {n}^{\mathrm {t}}\right)^{2} + \mathrm {g}_{\mathrm {rr}} \left( \mathrm {n}^{\mathrm {r}}\right)^{2} \tag {2 0}$$$$\mathbf {n} \cdot \mathbf {u} = 0 = \mathrm {n}^{\mathrm {t}} \mathrm {u}_{\mathrm {t}} + \mathrm {n}^{\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {u}_{\mathrm {r}}. \tag {2 1}$$Since$$\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u} = - 1 = \mathrm {g}^{\mathrm {tt}} \left( \mathrm {u}_{\mathrm {t}}\right)^{2} + \mathrm {g}^{\mathrm {rr}} \left( \mathrm {u}_{\mathrm {r}}\right)^{2} \tag {2 2}$$and$$\mathbf {g}^{\mathrm {rr}} = \left( \mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {rr}}\right)^{- 1} = - \left( \mathbf {g}^{\mathrm {tt}}\right)^{- 1} = - \mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {tt}} = 1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}$$the equations above imply that$$\mathrm {n}^{\mathrm {t}} = \mathrm {u}_{\mathrm {r}} , \quad \mathrm {n}^{\mathrm {r}} = - \mathrm {u}_{\mathrm {t}}. \tag {2 3}$$Let $i, j$ range over $\tau, \theta, \phi$ as before. Then Equation (17) holds for the exterior metric, since $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{in}} = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\mathrm{i}} = 0$ . Equation (18) holds for the exterior metric because $\mathbf{e}_{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\tau} = \mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} = - 1$ , $\mathbf{e}_{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\theta} = \mathbf{e}_{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\phi} = \mathbf{e}_{\theta} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\phi} = 0$ . Also,$$\begin{array}{l}\mathrm {K}_{\theta \theta}^{( + )} = \mathrm {K}_{\phi \phi}^{( + )} \sin^{- 2} \theta = - \frac {1} {2} ( \mathrm {r}^{2} )_{\mathrm {,n}} = - \frac {1} {2} ( \mathrm {r}^{2} )_{\mathrm {,r}} \mathrm {n}^{\mathrm {r}} \\= r u_{t} = - R \left( 1 - 2 M / R_{i}\right)^{\frac {1} {2}} \\= - \frac {1} {2} R_{i} ( 1 + \cos \eta ) ( 1 - 2 M / R_{i} )^{\frac {1} {2}} \\= - \frac {1} {2} a_{m} \sin \chi_{0} \cos \chi_{0} ( 1 + \cos \eta ) \\ \end{array}$$where we have used Equations (23), (13), (11) and (15). Thus $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{ij}}^{(+)} = \mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{ij}}^{(-)}$ and the proof is complete.
